title: Accelerating Netflix Data: A Cross-Team Journey from Offline to Online
gist: Netflix engineers Raj Ummadisetty and Ken Kurzweil detail CloudStream, a re-architecture that moves terabyte datasets from offline warehouses to online serving by converting them to RocksDB SSTables loaded directly onto stateful KV nodes — cutting deployment time up to 99% and cost 70%, guided by a 'confidence is currency' discipline of safety, observability, and validation.

insights.claim: Netflix anchors major architectural change in a 'confidence is currency' principle, earning stakeholder trust through three tenets: safety (the system protects itself), observability (it exposes internal state and progress), and validation (it proves it achieved intended results).
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: A transferable governance model for de-risking large migrations — the cultural precondition for the technical wins.

insights.claim: Adding progress reporting to the data-transfer system, initially omitted as too hard, turned out to be the single most effective enhancement — telling stakeholders 'X percent done' was a game changer for planning and trust.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Observability that quantifies progress converts stakeholder anxiety into planning capacity — a cheap, high-leverage investment.

insights.claim: Netflix's safety throttling, which scales migration up during off-peak and down during peak by tracking p90 read/write latencies, made migrations faster as a side effect of making them safer.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Safety mechanisms can be performance multipliers, not just brakes — a counterintuitive design lesson.
insights.locator: The safety mechanisms actually made us faster.

insights.claim: Only 16% of Netflix's Cassandra fleet is directly accessed by applications today and the number keeps falling, with the vast majority reached through abstraction layers handling around 70 million QPS.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Quantifies how deeply Netflix has decoupled apps from databases — the enabler for seamless backend swaps.
insights.quote: only 16% of our fleet is directly accessed by the applications today

insights.claim: Netflix's largest single abstraction handles around 8 million QPS, and its busiest backing Cassandra cluster serves about 1.8 million reads and 4.9 million writes per second.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Reference scale numbers for benchmarking distributed KV systems at hyperscale.

insights.claim: Netflix runs multiple purpose-built abstractions — key-value, entity (documents), time series, and GraphKV (property graphs) — with the KV abstraction operating as a distributed HashMap-as-a-service two-level map.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: A menu of data abstractions by access pattern — a model for platform teams centralizing storage decisions.
insights.locator: At Netflix, we have multiple abstractions

insights.claim: The abstraction layer was seeded when Cassandra deprecated the Thrift protocol in version 3.0; Netflix convinced teams to do 'one last migration' to the KV interface in exchange for being shielded from all future backend migrations.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Forced-migration events are the wedge to introduce abstraction layers — a repeatable platform adoption play.
insights.locator: We did not always have abstractions.

insights.claim: The abstraction layer adds a latency hop, but Netflix found the added latency unimportant relative to the benefits, and builds idempotency tokens into all mutating APIs so requests stay idempotent across different backends.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: The abstraction tax is worth paying when it buys backend flexibility and correctness guarantees.

insights.claim: The traditional approach of using Spark batch jobs to write online records let teams effectively DDoS their own applications — the 'noisy neighbor' problem — and collapsed entirely on terabyte datasets, taking days to load despite large spend.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Names the failure mode driving the re-architecture — batch-into-online contention is a common, costly anti-pattern.
insights.quote: We know this as the noisy neighbor problem.

insights.claim: By recognizing that immutable, read-only datasets don't need Cassandra for consensus, Netflix could convert them to RocksDB SSTables and load them directly onto KV nodes — doing the I/O-heavy file generation entirely offline to remove online contention.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Exploiting access patterns (immutable vs mutable) unlocks a cheaper serving path — the core architectural insight.

insights.claim: Netflix chose RocksDB over Hollow, SQLite, Cassandra SSTables, and Memcached's ext format using a matrix on support, external-consumption design, and compaction — SQLite fit everything except it can't compact two files, while RocksDB offered online compaction and mounting.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: The selection criteria (online compaction, external format, mountability) are a reusable checklist for embedded-store choices.

insights.claim: Netflix treats a proof of concept as the most effective way to convert skeptics: 'A POC is worth 100 meetings,' with an undeniable working prototype turning non-believers into believers.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Cultural lever for pushing contested architecture through an org — build it rather than debate it.

insights.claim: A 'Pathfinder' is software intentionally built to be thrown away: it unblocks a business-critical use case for a stakeholder willing to take risk while letting the team discover unknown unknowns before building the hardened product.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: A disciplined disposable-prototype pattern — deliberate throwaway software as a de-risking and learning vehicle.
insights.quote: A Pathfinder is software that you intentionally plan to migrate from.

insights.claim: The Pathfinder cut dataset load time to 27 minutes (later 40 after hardening) versus the several days it took to load a multimillion-dollar Cassandra cluster the traditional way.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Order-of-magnitude proof that the offline-SSTable path works — the number that justified full productization.
insights.quote: we got that down to 27 minutes

insights.claim: Netflix deliberately chose its largest, mission-critical use case first for the Pathfinder — 'eat the frog' — reasoning that solving the hardest case guarantees the general case is covered.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Counter to MVP-easy-case instinct: tackling the stretch case first prevents nasty surprises in general rollout.
insights.quote: if you eat the frog at the beginning of the day, you don't have to eat it the rest of the day

insights.claim: Moving KV from stateless to stateful required new control components — a partition group manager issuing leases, a central routing manager, and a per-cluster routing layer — because each server now stores a unique subset of data rather than being interchangeable.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: The architectural cost of statefulness (routing, leasing) is the price of the SSTable performance gains.

insights.claim: The old hours-long 'sawtooth' ingestion gave teams a window to catch regressions, but the new near-instant switch raises blast radius, making a dedicated validation staging stage — where teams run a validator app before promoting — critical.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Speed removes a natural safety buffer; faster deploys demand explicit pre-promotion validation gates.
insights.locator: If you recall, the traditional approach to data ingestion

insights.claim: For massive datasets that traditionally took days and cost millions annually, Netflix now loads in under 40 minutes and switches in seconds — a 99% reduction in deployment time and 70% cost savings.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: The headline ROI number for the re-architecture — a benchmark for offline-to-online data movement projects.
insights.quote: 99% reduction in the deployment time and 70% in cost savings

insights.claim: For high-velocity versioned feature data, Netflix cut load times to under 10 minutes with 90% operational savings and gained the previously unavailable ability to roll back and roll forward between dataset versions.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Versioned rollback/forward for datasets is a distinct capability enabling safer ML feature deployment.
insights.locator: The other use case involved high-velocity versioned feature data

insights.claim: Netflix generalized the work into a reusable Capture-Conversion-Deployment framework whose decoupled phases let separate teams optimize independently and enable straightforward roll-forward/back to previous datasets.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Modular data-movement architecture is the durable IP — components (e.g., Cassandra SSTable conversion) plug in without disruption.
insights.locator: Generalizing the Architecture - Capture, Conversion, Deployment

insights.claim: Netflix is extending the approach to mutable datasets by converting captures to Cassandra SSTables via the Apache Cassandra analytics project and loading them into live clusters, and can now export SSTables to S3 for backups and migrate behind the abstraction to any datastore, such as Cassandra to DynamoDB.
insights.speaker: Rajasekhar Ummadisetty
insights.so_what: Backend portability (Cassandra-to-DynamoDB) behind one abstraction is a cost/performance optionality worth tracking.
